subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: It is the second largest producer of coal in the world. Q: India has vast mineral resources. R: It also has significant reserves of iron ore and bauxite. S: The mining sector contributes substantially to the economy.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The Industrial Revolution transformed manufacturing processes. Q: It began in England in the late 18th century. R: Steam engines powered factories and railways. S: This led to unprecedented economic growth.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Climate change is one of the biggest challenges today. Q: Rising temperatures cause extreme weather events. R: Governments worldwide are taking mitigation measures. S: However, collective action at individual level is also needed.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The automobile industry is shifting towards electric vehicles. Q: Battery technology has improved significantly in recent years. R: Charging infrastructure is being expanded rapidly. S: This transition will reduce carbon emissions.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Coal is formed over millions of years. Q: Under heat and pressure, peat transforms into coal. R: Dead plant material accumulates in swamps forming peat. S: This process is called coalification.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The internet has revolutionised communication. Q: People can now connect instantly across the globe. R: Social media platforms enable sharing of information. S: However, digital divide remains a concern.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Water scarcity is affecting many regions of India. Q: Groundwater levels are depleting at alarming rates. R: Efficient irrigation techniques can help conserve water. S: Rainwater harvesting is also being promoted.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Natural disasters cause widespread destruction. Q: Early warning systems can reduce the impact. R: Preparedness and planning are essential. S: Governments invest in disaster management infrastructure.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Education is the key to personal and social development. Q: It empowers individuals to make informed decisions. R: Quality education should be accessible to all. S: Governments must invest in educational infrastructure.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Democracy is a system of government by the people. Q: Citizens elect their representatives through free and fair elections. R: These representatives make laws on behalf of the people. S: An independent judiciary ensures that laws are followed.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Renewable energy sources are gaining importance. Q: Solar and wind power are the most promising. R: Their cost has decreased dramatically. S: Many countries have set ambitious renewable energy targets.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The Earth's atmosphere is composed of several layers. Q: The troposphere is the lowest layer where weather occurs. R: Above it lies the stratosphere containing the ozone layer. S: The mesosphere and thermosphere extend further upward.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Artificial intelligence is transforming industries. Q: Machine learning algorithms analyse vast amounts of data. R: AI-powered systems can make predictions and recommendations. S: Ethical considerations around AI are being debated.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Biodiversity is essential for ecosystem health. Q: Each species plays a unique role in its habitat. R: Loss of biodiversity threatens ecosystem stability. S: Conservation efforts are crucial to protect endangered species.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The space race began in the mid-20th century. Q: The Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first satellite. R: The US responded with the Apollo program. S: This competition led to significant technological advances.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Stock markets reflect the health of an economy. Q: Share prices fluctuate based on supply and demand. R: Investors buy and sell shares of publicly listed companies. S: Market regulators ensure fair trading practices.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Urbanisation is increasing rapidly worldwide. Q: People migrate to cities in search of better opportunities. R: This puts pressure on housing and infrastructure. S: Sustainable urban planning is needed to manage growth.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The human brain is a complex organ. Q: It contains billions of neurons that transmit signals. R: Different regions control different functions. S: Neuroplasticity allows the brain to adapt and reorganise.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Sustainable development balances economic growth and environmental protection. Q: The concept gained global attention after the Brundtland Report. R: It emphasises meeting present needs without compromising future generations. S: The UN Sustainable Development Goals outline specific targets.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems. Q: They provide habitat for numerous marine species. R: Climate change and ocean acidification threaten their survival. S: Conservation programmes are working to protect and restore reefs.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Inflation reduces the purchasing power of money. Q: Central banks adjust interest rates to control inflation. R: High inflation can erode savings and reduce living standards. S: Moderate inflation is considered healthy for economic growth.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The printing press revolutionised the spread of information. Q: It was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the 15th century. R: Books became more affordable and widely available. S: This led to increased literacy rates across Europe.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Vaccines have saved millions of lives worldwide. Q: They work by training the immune system to fight diseases. R: Herd immunity protects those who cannot be vaccinated. S: Vaccine development requires rigorous clinical trials.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Open-pit mining is used when coal seams are near the surface. Q: The topsoil and overburden are removed first. R: The coal is then extracted using heavy machinery. S: After mining, the land is reclaimed for other uses.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The Amazon rainforest is often called the lungs of the Earth. Q: It produces about 20% of the world's oxygen. R: Deforestation rates have increased in recent decades. S: International efforts are underway to protect the rainforest.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Globalisation has connected economies around the world. Q: International trade has grown exponentially. R: Multinational corporations operate across borders. S: Critics argue that globalisation increases inequality.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates shift suddenly. Q: The point of origin is called the epicentre. R: Seismic waves travel through the Earth's crust. S: The Richter scale measures the magnitude of earthquakes.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: The Green Revolution increased agricultural productivity. Q: High-yield crop varieties were developed and introduced. R: Chemical fertilisers and pesticides were used extensively. S: This helped many countries achieve food security.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Cyber security is a growing concern for organisations. Q: Data breaches can result in significant financial losses. R: Companies invest in firewalls and encryption technologies. S: Employee training is essential to prevent phishing attacks.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem: Rearrange: P: Shakespeare is widely regarded as the greatest writer in English. Q: He wrote 37 plays and 154 sonnets. R: His works explore universal themes of love, power, and betrayal. S: His influence on the English language is immeasurable.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

According to the passage, India has the ____ largest coal reserves in the world.

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem:
Passage: Underground mining is one of the most hazardous occupations in the world. The primary risks include roof falls, gas explosions, flooding, and ventilation failures. In India, the Mines Act of 1952 and the Coal Mines Regulations of 2017 govern safety standards. Modern technology has significantly improved mine safety through real-time monitoring systems, gas detectors, and automated equipment. Despite these advances, safety remains a critical concern, and regular training programs are mandatory for all mine workers. The introduction of continuous miners and longwall mining methods has increased both productivity and safety.

Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a risk in underground mining?

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Gondwana coal fields are located in which part of India?

Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

Coal grading is primarily based on:

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

The Industrial Revolution began in which country?

What powered steam engines during the Industrial Revolution?

Which of the following was a social cost of the Industrial Revolution?
options: Poor working conditions and child labour
Poor working conditions and child labour
solution: Poor working conditions and child labour

subject: Paper-I · General English
stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

India's energy demand is expected to grow at ____ % annually.

subject: Paper-I · General English
